summary: This lesson introduces the verb ser (to be for characteristics), contrasting it with estar (to be for states). All five conjugated forms of ser are taught, along with the e-to-es prosthesis pattern in Spanish words that begin with s+consonant in English.

teachings.summary: Spanish cannot begin a word with s+consonant (st-, sp-, sc-, etc.), so an e- is added to the beginning. This explains the difference between English 'state' and Spanish 'estar', and applies to many cognates.
teachings.rule: When an English word begins with s+consonant, the Spanish cognate will have an e- prefix.
teachings.notes: The o-to-ue split also applies in escuela (school).

teachings.summary: Ser is used for characteristics of being, as opposed to estar which is used for states. Ser by itself means 'being' (un ser = a being). All five present-tense forms are taught: soy, es, son, somos, eres.
teachings.rule: Ser conjugation: soy (I), eres (you informal), es (he/she/it/you formal), somos (we), son (they/you guys). Only four verbs use -oy for I: voy, doy, estoy, soy.
teachings.notes: Eres is the most irregular-looking form. The phrase '¿De dónde eres?' can help remember it. Somos is derived from son by removing the -n and adding -mos.

teachings.summary: Ser is for characteristics (what someone/something is like), estar is for states (how someone/something is doing). The question '¿Cómo está?' asks how someone is doing, while '¿Cómo es?' asks what someone is like.
teachings.rule: Use estar for states (how is he doing: ¿Cómo está?) and ser for characteristics (what is he like: ¿Cómo es?).

examples.english: How is he? (How is he doing?)
How is he? (What is he like?)

teachings.summary: Ustedes is the plural of usted but is not necessarily formal. In most of the Spanish-speaking world (outside parts of Spain), ustedes is simply how you say 'you guys' without any formal connotation.
teachings.rule: Ustedes uses the same verb form as ellos/ellas (son, están, etc.).
teachings.notes: Only in some parts of Spain is ustedes considered formal in the plural.

vocabulary.derivation: derived from son by removing -n and adding -mos
